---
title: "Command-Line Programs"
description: "Run scripts and accept command-line arguments with argparse."
execute:
  echo: true
---

## Why this matters

Run scripts and accept command-line arguments with argparse.

This lesson combines related subtopics that belong together in one learning
conversation. You will still pause for a quiz after each section, but you do not
need to jump between separate pages while building one mental model.

::: {.questions}
### Questions

By the end of this lesson, you should be able to answer:

- How do the sections in **Command-Line Programs** fit together?
- Which small example demonstrates each section?
- Which debugging clue should I check first for each section?
:::

::: {.learning-objectives}
### Learning objectives

You will practice how to:

- explain the shared concept for this lesson;
- use each section as one step in a larger workflow;
- complete 2 short section quizzes before moving on;
- connect examples, mistakes, and debugging routines.
:::

## Lesson map

- Section 1: **Command-Line Scripts** — Run Python files as repeatable command-line programs.
- Section 2: **argparse** — Accept command-line options and arguments clearly.

## Section 1: Command-Line Scripts

Run Python files as repeatable command-line programs.

::: {.analogy}
### Analogy for this section

A script is a small appliance: give it input, press run, and receive output.
:::

### Mental model

A script is a Python file designed to be executed from the terminal.

### Tiny example

Predict what will happen before you run the code.

```bash
python scripts/hello.py
python -m my_project
```

### Walkthrough

1. `python scripts/hello.py` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
2. `python -m my_project` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.

After running the example, compare the actual output with your prediction. If
they differ, do not erase your prediction. The difference is the part that can
teach you the most.

### Challenge

::: {.challenge}
Change one input value, predict the new output, run the code, and explain the difference
in one sentence.
:::

<details class="solution">
<summary>Show one possible solution path</summary>

1. Copy the tiny example into Colab, Jupyter, or a `.py` file.
2. Mark the line you plan to change.
3. Write a one-sentence prediction.
4. Run the changed code.
5. If the result surprises you, restore the original and change a smaller part.

The goal is not to find the only correct answer. The goal is to create a small
experiment where you can explain cause and effect.

</details>

### Common mistakes

::: {.debugging-corner}
If a script depends on a relative file path, running from a different folder can break
it.
:::

When you get stuck, use this debugging routine:

1. Read the last line of the error message or inspect the unexpected output.
2. Find the smallest line of code that could be responsible.
3. Print or inspect the value and type at that point.
4. Change one thing.
5. Run again and record what changed.

### Section checkpoint

This quiz checks the ideas in this section before you move on.

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "projects-and-environments-command-line-scripts",
  "title": "Command-Line Scripts checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "main-idea",
      "prompt": "Which statement best describes Command-Line Scripts?",
      "options": [
        "A script is a Python file designed to be executed from the terminal.",
        "It is mainly about memorizing every Python command before writing code.",
        "It is a topic you can safely skip because later tools replace it.",
        "It is only useful after you already know advanced programming."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The key idea is: A script is a Python file designed to be executed from the terminal."
    },
    {
      "id": "analogy",
      "prompt": "What is the purpose of the analogy in this lesson?",
      "options": [
        "To connect a new programming idea to an everyday experience.",
        "To replace practice with memorization.",
        "To prove that programming is only for experts.",
        "To avoid reading and running code examples."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The analogy in this lesson is: A script is a small appliance: give it input, press run, and receive output."
    },
    {
      "id": "debugging-habit",
      "prompt": "What is the best first debugging habit for this topic?",
      "options": [
        "Read the message or output carefully and change one thing at a time.",
        "Delete the whole program immediately.",
        "Change many lines before running again.",
        "Assume Python is random."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Small experiments make cause and effect visible."
    },
    {
      "id": "practice-habit",
      "prompt": "How should you practice after the tiny example works?",
      "options": [
        "Make one small variation, predict the result, run it, and explain what changed.",
        "Copy a large project without reading it.",
        "Skip practice and rely on recognition.",
        "Only read the final key points."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Prediction plus small variation builds durable understanding."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## Section 2: argparse

Accept command-line options and arguments clearly.

::: {.analogy}
### Analogy for this section

argparse is a receptionist for your script: it asks for the right information and
explains the rules.
:::

### Mental model

argparse parses terminal text into named Python values and generates help messages.

### Tiny example

Predict what will happen before you run the code.

```python
import argparse

parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
parser.add_argument("name")
args = parser.parse_args()
print(f"Hello, {args.name}")
```

### Walkthrough

1. `import argparse` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
2. `parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
3. `parser.add_argument("name")` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
4. `args = parser.parse_args()` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
5. `print(f"Hello, {args.name}")` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.

After running the example, compare the actual output with your prediction. If
they differ, do not erase your prediction. The difference is the part that can
teach you the most.

### Challenge

::: {.challenge}
Change one input value, predict the new output, run the code, and explain the difference
in one sentence.
:::

<details class="solution">
<summary>Show one possible solution path</summary>

1. Copy the tiny example into Colab, Jupyter, or a `.py` file.
2. Mark the line you plan to change.
3. Write a one-sentence prediction.
4. Run the changed code.
5. If the result surprises you, restore the original and change a smaller part.

The goal is not to find the only correct answer. The goal is to create a small
experiment where you can explain cause and effect.

</details>

### Common mistakes

::: {.debugging-corner}
Command-line arguments are strings by default. Use type=int when you need numbers.
:::

When you get stuck, use this debugging routine:

1. Read the last line of the error message or inspect the unexpected output.
2. Find the smallest line of code that could be responsible.
3. Print or inspect the value and type at that point.
4. Change one thing.
5. Run again and record what changed.

### Section checkpoint

This quiz checks the ideas in this section before you move on.

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "projects-and-environments-argparse",
  "title": "argparse checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "main-idea",
      "prompt": "Which statement best describes argparse?",
      "options": [
        "argparse parses terminal text into named Python values and generates help messages.",
        "It is mainly about memorizing every Python command before writing code.",
        "It is a topic you can safely skip because later tools replace it.",
        "It is only useful after you already know advanced programming."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The key idea is: argparse parses terminal text into named Python values and generates help messages."
    },
    {
      "id": "analogy",
      "prompt": "What is the purpose of the analogy in this lesson?",
      "options": [
        "To connect a new programming idea to an everyday experience.",
        "To replace practice with memorization.",
        "To prove that programming is only for experts.",
        "To avoid reading and running code examples."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The analogy in this lesson is: argparse is a receptionist for your script: it asks for the right information and explains the rules."
    },
    {
      "id": "debugging-habit",
      "prompt": "What is the best first debugging habit for this topic?",
      "options": [
        "Read the message or output carefully and change one thing at a time.",
        "Delete the whole program immediately.",
        "Change many lines before running again.",
        "Assume Python is random."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Small experiments make cause and effect visible."
    },
    {
      "id": "practice-habit",
      "prompt": "How should you practice after the tiny example works?",
      "options": [
        "Make one small variation, predict the result, run it, and explain what changed.",
        "Copy a large project without reading it.",
        "Skip practice and rely on recognition.",
        "Only read the final key points."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Prediction plus small variation builds durable understanding."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## Notebook and Colab practice

Open a blank notebook at <https://colab.new>. Use one section at a time: copy the
tiny example, predict the result, run it, answer the section quiz, and then move
to the next section. This is better than copying the entire page at once.

## Instructor note

<details class="instructor">
<summary>Teaching notes</summary>

- Treat each section as a short teaching episode.
- Pause for the section quiz before introducing the next section.
- Ask learners to compare sections: what stayed the same, and what changed?
- If time is short, teach the first two sections live and assign the rest as practice.

</details>

## Key points

::: {.key-points}
- **Command-Line Scripts**: A script is a Python file designed to be executed from the terminal.
- **argparse**: argparse parses terminal text into named Python values and generates help messages.
- Use the section quizzes as gates: review before moving on if a quiz feels uncertain.
:::

## References

- Python modules tutorial: https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/modules.html
- venv documentation: https://docs.python.org/3/library/venv.html
- argparse documentation: https://docs.python.org/3/library/argparse.html
- Python Tutorial: https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/
- Quarto OJS documentation: https://quarto.org/docs/interactive/ojs/
- ipywidgets documentation: https://ipywidgets.readthedocs.io/en/stable/
